We also see associations on a much broader physiological level.
Large long-term studies show that every additional 10 grams of fiber per day
is associated with a measurable reduction in certain intestinal cell abnormalities.
Intake levels around 25 to 30 grams daily are consistently linked to more protective long-term health outcomes.
Emerging evidence also points toward benefits in areas like immune-related skin conditions and the inflammatory regulation.
And very important, this does not mean fiber is a cure or a treatment.
But it does show something fundamental.
Fiber influences the biological environment in which our cells function.
So let us now look at where we actually find fiber in everyday life.
And the good news is, fiber is not something exotic.
It is naturally present in everyday plant foods.
Like vegetables, legumes, oats, berries, fruits, nuts, seeds and whole grains.
Some contain more soluble fiber, others more insoluble fiber.
But most plant foods contain a combination of both.
And because fiber exists exclusively in plants, it also fits naturally in plant-based and vegan nutrition concepts.
But fiber is not only about quantity.
Diversity matters too.
Different plants contain different fiber structures and different bacteria inside the microbiome prefer different types of fuel.
So one of the simplest but most powerful nutritional messages may simply be this.
Eat more plants.
Eat more diversity.
And give your God something to work with.
